summary: Stancil argues that analytics teams overinvest in dashboards and BI systems that produce fleeting value while neglecting the ad hoc analyses that drive the most important decisions. The real problem is that these analyses are ungoverned, hard to find later, and treated as disposable. He proposes reframing and organizing this work—starting by renaming it—to make it central, durable, and systematized.
money_quotes: We invest in the ephemeral, and throw away the enduring.
No amount of “reimagining the deck chair” will keep our Titanic afloat.
By naming it something temporary, we make it temporary.
A system that organizes some results and loses others isn’t organized; ungoverned work anywhere undermines governance everywhere.
So, by all means, let’s stop building dashboards. But first, to convince everyone—including ourselves—of the value of what we should be building instead, let’s come up with some better technobabble.
key_insight: Dashboards are a seductive but low‑durability investment; the real value comes from ad hoc analysis, which needs to be renamed, systematized, and preserved to build lasting organizational knowledge.
